perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be interpreted according to general business customs. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to its principles of conflict of laws.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to the section about 'general business customs' may obscure the explicit legal framework intended to govern the agreement, potentially leading to misinterpretations or challenges based on customary practices rather than Delaware law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Contract Law - unambiguous contract interpretation
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law prioritizes the plain meaning of the contract language. By placing the governing law clause within a context suggesting interpretation based on 'general business customs', it creates ambiguity and potentially contradicts the principle that the agreement should be primarily interpreted based on Delaware law.
perturbation.location: Entire document, implied by recontextualization of governing law clause
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 6 > Chapter 27 Authenticated PDF CONTRACTS Subchapter I. General Provisions Subchapter II. Statute of Frauds and Perjuries Subchapter III. Equipment Dealer Contracts Subchapter IV. Consumer Contracts
perturbation.justification: The perturbation introduces a 'Structural Flaw Legal Contradiction' by placing the governing law clause within the context of 'general business customs.' This placement creates ambiguity, potentially contradicting the principle that the agreement should be primarily interpreted under Delaware law. This aligns with the definition of Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction, where a required legal disclosure is placed in a misleading section, affecting its clarity. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides a general reference to Delaware contract law, the core of the contradiction lies in the structural placement, making the intended legal framework unclear, which is a flaw in contract structure and therefore the legal obligation is diminished.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written, between the parties with respect to such subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement is subject to change at any time, and any modifications to the agreement should be noted somewhere in the margin, although these modifications are not formally integrated into the document itself. This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties with respect to the subject matter hereof and supersedes all prior or contemporaneous communications and proposals, whether oral or written, between the parties with respect to such subject matter.
perturbation.explanation: The original text stipulates that this is the entire agreement, but by adding that this agreement is subject to change any time, creates a legal contradiction and ambiguity, since this could be contested in court, especially because it suggests that marginal notes are sufficient to change the whole agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law - Modification of Contract Terms
perturbation.law_explanation: According to UCC § 2-209, an agreement modifying a contract needs no consideration to be binding but must meet the test of good faith. The added statement makes the agreement subject to change 'at any time' with just marginal notes, which is contrary to standard contract modification requiring mutual agreement and proper documentation, potentially hindering its enforceability.
perturbation.location: Entire document, implied by recontextualization of entire agreement clause
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-209. Modification, Rescission and Waiver. (1) An agreement modifying a contract within this Article needs no consideration to be binding. (2) A signed agreement which excludes modification or rescission except by a signed writing cannot be otherwise modified or rescinded, but except as between merchants such a requirement on a form supplied by the merchant must be separately signed by the other party. (3) The requirements of the statute of frauds section of this Article (Section 2-201 ) must be satisfied if the contract as modified is within its provisions. (4)Although an attempt at modification or rescission does not satisfy the requirements of subsection (2) or (3) it can operate as a waiver. (5) A party who has made a waiver affecting an executory portion of the contract may retract the waiver by reasonable notification received by the other party that strict performance will be required of any term waived, unless the retraction would be unjust in view of a material change of position in reliance on the waiver. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: rship that put all the component commercial laws together in a comprehensive Uniform Commercial Code that was offered to the states for their consideration in 1951. Pennsylvania became the first state to adopt the UCC in 1953, and every other state followed suit over the next twenty years. News ULC Statement on Ownership of Investment Property under Uniform Commercial Code Article 8 ULC Statement on Central Bank Digital Currencies and the Uniform Commercial Code Cooperation with ALI The UCC is a joint project of the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute. Recognizing that drafting a combined commercial code was a massive undertaking, the ULC invited ALI to participate in the codification project, and the ALI board accepted the invitation in 1942. Over the next ten years the two organizations collaborated at drafting meetings funded in large part by a generous grant from the Maurice and Laura Falk Foundation, with additional funding contributed from law firms, banks, and businesses that recognized the need for uniform commercial laws. The UCC Today The UCC is maintained under the guidance of the Permanent Editorial Board for the Uniform Commercial Code (PEB) , comprised of members appointed by the ULC and the ALI. The PEB, established in 1961, monitors developments in commercial law, recommends UCC amendments and revisions when necessary, and publishes official commentary to help courts interpret specific UCC provisions. An endowment established with the original Falk Foundation grant funding and replenished with UCC publishing royalties is available to fund UCC drafting projects. Articles Article 1, General Provisions Uniform Commercial Code Article 1 contains definitions and general provisions applicable as default rules to transactions covered under other articles of the UCC. Article 1 was last revised in 2001, with a few minor amendments since then to harmonize with recent revisions of other UCC articles. View Article 1, General Provisions Article 2, Sales Uniform Commercial Code Article 2 governs the sale of goods. It was part of the original Uniform Commercial Code approved in 1951. Article 2 represented a revision and modernization of the Uniform Sales Act, which was originally approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1906. The Uniform Law Commission and American Law Institute approved a revised Article 2 in 2003 that was not adopted in any state, and was subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus the 1951 version of Article 2 is the most recent official version. View Article 2, Sales Article 2A, Leases Uniform Commercial Code Article 2A governs leases of personal property. It was first added to the Uniform Commercial Code in 1987 and amended in 1990. A revision was approved by the Uniform Law Commission and the American Law Institute in 2003, but was not adopted in any jurisdiction and subsequently withdrawn by both organizations in 2011. Thus, the 1987 version of Article 2A, as amended in 1990, remains the official text. View Article 2A, Leases Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Uniform Commercial Code Article 3 governs negotiable instruments: drafts (including checks) and notes representing a promise to pay a sum of money, and that have independent value because they are negotiable. An instrument is negotiable if it can be transferred to another person and remain enforceable against the person who originally made the promise to pay. The substance of Article 3 has its roots in the Negotiable Instrument Law first approved by the National Conference of Commissioners on Uniform State Laws in 1896. That early uniform law was revised and incorporated into the original version of the UCC in 1951, and a further revision was approved in 1990. Finally, a set of amendments to UCC Articles 3 and 4 was approved in 2002. View Article 3, Negotiable Instruments Article 4, Bank Deposits and Collections Uniform Commercial Code Article 4 governs bank deposits and collecti
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a significant ambiguity regarding contract modifications, potentially contradicting established legal principles for contract amendments. While UCC § 2-209(1) states that 'An agreement modifying a contract within this Article needs no consideration to be binding,' the added clause suggesting changes can be made 'at any time' via marginal notes undermines the requirement for clear agreement and proper documentation of contract modifications. This contradicts the standard practice for modifying contracts, as it suggests informal amendments are sufficient, which could lead to disputes and hinder enforceability. The snippet highlights the need for a formal agreement, even if consideration isn't required, but the modification opens the door for unmanaged changes, contradicting the need for clarity in contracts.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party, the exact amount of which may be difficult to ascertain. Therefore, the Receiving Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to prevent or curtail any such unauthorized disclosure or use, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party acknowledges that unauthorized disclosure or use of the Confidential Information may cause irreparable harm to the Disclosing Party, the exact amount of which may be difficult to ascertain. Therefore, the Receiving Party agrees that the Disclosing Party shall be entitled to seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction to prevent or curtail any such unauthorized disclosure or use, in addition to any other remedies available at law or in equity; however, any legal action must commence with a period of informal mediation and the Disclosing Party should take this as a non-binding recommendation.
perturbation.explanation: The final sentence's inclusion of 'any legal action must commence with a period of informal mediation and the Disclosing Party should take this as a non-binding recommendation' dilutes the right to seek injunctive relief. While mediation is often encouraged, framing it as a requirement, even a non-binding one, before injunctive relief undermines the immediacy and urgency typically associated with seeking injunctive relief for protecting confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: Under Delaware law (and generally), injunctive relief is designed to provide immediate equitable remedies to prevent irreparable harm. By suggesting mandatory informal mediation before seeking injunctive relief, the modified text introduces a delay that might render the injunctive relief ineffective, contradicting the purpose of immediate protection against disclosure of confidential information. This weakens the enforceability of the injunctive relief provision.
perturbation.location: Entire document, implied by recontextualization of injunctive relief clause

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a mandatory informal mediation period before seeking injunctive relief, which undermines the immediacy and effectiveness of such relief, thus creating a structural flaw that results in a legal contradiction. The Law Explanation highlights this, stating that injunctive relief is intended to provide immediate remedies, while the mediation requirement delays this. The original contract allowed immediate action, which the changed version impairs. Because Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 404 and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is not helpful, the decision is based on the Law Explanation in conjunction with the Perturbation Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement and the rights and obligations hereunder shall not be assignable or otherwise transferable by the Receiving Party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. Any attempted assignment or transfer in violation of this Section shall be void.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement and the rights and obligations hereunder shall not be assignable or otherwise transferable by the Receiving Party without the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party. Any attempted assignment or transfer in violation of this Section shall be void, but, in the spirit of cooperation, the Disclosing Party should consider allowing assignment if it benefits the Receiving Party in some way, even if that benefit is not apparent to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: The added clause saying '... the Disclosing Party should consider allowing assignment if it benefits the Receiving Party in some way, even if that benefit is not apparent to the Disclosing Party' directly contradicts the previous sentence by adding uncertainty and weakening the prohibition of assignment.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 317 defines the rights of an assignor and assignee. The original clause firmly prevents assignment without consent. The added clause introduces ambiguity by suggesting that the Disclosing Party 'should consider' allowing assignment even if it benefits only the Receiving Party, undermining the clear prohibition of assignment without explicit written consent.
perturbation.location: Entire document, implied by recontextualization of assignment clause
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: third party beneficiary | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you third party beneficiary A third party beneficiary is a person benefiting from a contract made between two other parties, where the two contracting parties intended to benefit the third party beneficiary. The third party beneficiary is not a party to the contract, but has rights under the contract since it was made with an intent to benefit them. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Keywords contract law Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The original text creates a firm prohibition on assignment or transfer without the Disclosing Party's written consent. The changed text weakens this prohibition by suggesting the Disclosing Party 'should consider' allowing assignment if it benefits the Receiving Party, even if the benefit is not apparent to the Disclosing Party. This introduces ambiguity and undermines the original clear prohibition, creating a legal contradiction as it makes the enforceability of the non-assignment clause uncertain.

perturbation.original_text: No failure or delay by a party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right, power or privilege.
perturbation.changed_text: No failure or delay by a party in exercising any right, power or privilege hereunder shall operate as a waiver thereof, nor shall any single or partial exercise thereof preclude any other or further exercise thereof or the exercise of any other right, power or privilege, however, consistent laxity in enforcement should be taken as a sign that said power has been silently waved.
perturbation.explanation: The addition of the clause that 'consistent laxity in enforcement should be taken as a sign that said power has been silently waved' directly contradicts the intent of the clause. The clause is meant to explicitly state that delays in enforcement do not constitute a waiver; the addition states that delays can be considered a silent waiver. This makes the clause contradictory and confusing.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law recognizes the concept of waiver as the voluntary relinquishment of a known right. The original no-waiver clause explicitly states that a party's failure to enforce a right does not constitute a waiver of that right. The addition implies that consistent laxity in enforcement can be interpreted as a silent waiver, directly contradicting the express terms of the no-waiver clause and creating uncertainty regarding the parties' rights and obligations.
perturbation.location: Entire document, implied by recontextualization of no-waiver clause
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: waiver | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you waiver A waiver is the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege . In legal contexts, a waiver is often documented in writing, but it can also be implied through actions . By waiving a right, an individual or party chooses not to enforce a particular right, claim , or provision they are legally entitled to. [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex accidents and injuries COMMERCE commercial activities LIFE EVENTS accidents & injuries (tort law) THE LEGAL PROCESS courts criminal procedure business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure individual rights legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction to the original 'no waiver' clause. The original text explicitly states that a failure to enforce a right does not constitute a waiver. The added clause, however, suggests that consistent laxity can be interpreted as a silent waiver. This directly undermines the purpose of the no-waiver clause, creating a legal contradiction. The scraped law snippet defines a waiver as 'the intentional or voluntary relinquishment or surrender of a recognized right or privilege' and states that it can be 'implied through actions'. The added clause implies a waiver through inaction ('consistent laxity'), directly contradicting the original clause's intent to prevent such interpretation. This falls under the Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction definition because the added clause impacts clarity and enforceability.
